title: Payment gateway returns 503 during peak hours causing lost transactions
body:
## Description

During peak traffic (usually 11am-2pm EST), the Stripe gateway intermittently returns HTTP 503. Our current implementation treats this as a permanent failure and marks the payment as failed.

## Expected Behavior

Transient 503 errors should be retried with backoff before marking payment as failed.

## Impact

We're losing approximately 2-3% of transactions during peak hours. Customer support is receiving 15+ tickets/day about failed payments that succeed on manual retry.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Retry up to 3 times with exponential backoff
- [ ] Add jitter to prevent thundering herd
- [ ] Log each retry attempt for observability
- [ ] Only mark as failed after all retries exhausted
author: product-team

comments.author: alice.chen
comments.body: I've confirmed the 503s are transient — they resolve within 2-5 seconds. Exponential backoff with base 2s should handle this well. Will also add a jitter of 0-1s to avoid retry storms.

comments.author: bob.martinez
comments.body: Should we consider a circuit breaker instead? If the gateway is consistently failing, we don't want to keep hammering it.

comments.author: alice.chen
comments.body: Good point, but the failures are isolated per-request, not systemic. A circuit breaker would block healthy requests. Documented this decision in ADR-017.

title: Race condition: duplicate charges when payment retried concurrently
body:
## Description

When a payment times out and the client retries, two workers can pick up the same payment simultaneously. Both see state='pending' and attempt to charge, resulting in a double charge.

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Submit payment request
2. First request times out (but is still processing on backend)
3. Client retries immediately
4. Both the original and retry process the payment

## Root Cause Analysis

The `process_payment` function reads state and charges in separate steps without any locking mechanism. TOCTOU vulnerability.

## Proposed Solution

Add optimistic locking with a version counter on the payment record. The transition to 'processing' state should be atomic.
author: alice.chen

comments.author: bob.martinez
comments.body: I think we should extract the state transitions into a proper state machine first. The current process_payment is doing too much — state management, side effects, and error handling all in one 200-line function.

comments.author: carol.nguyen
comments.body: Agreed on the state machine extraction. Also, should we add idempotency keys from the client side? That way even if our internal locking fails, we have a second layer of protection.

comments.author: alice.chen
comments.body: Both good ideas. I'll do the state machine refactor first (makes the locking fix cleaner), then add idempotency keys as a separate PR. Let's use Redis for idempotency storage with 24h TTL.

title: Add payment webhook signature verification
body:
## Description

We currently accept all incoming webhook payloads without verifying the signature header. This means anyone who discovers our webhook endpoint could send fake payment confirmations.

## Security Impact

Critical — an attacker could mark payments as completed without actual money transfer.

## Requirements

- Verify HMAC-SHA256 signature on all incoming webhooks
- Reject requests with missing or invalid signatures with 401
- Add timing-safe comparison to prevent timing attacks
author: security-team

comments.author: dave.kim
comments.body: I'll handle this. We should use `hmac.compare_digest()` for the timing-safe comparison. Also need to handle the case where the webhook secret rotates — should we support multiple valid secrets during rotation?

title: Payment reconciliation report shows stale data
body:
## Description

The daily reconciliation report is reading from a read replica that's up to 30 seconds behind. During peak hours, this means recently completed payments show as 'processing', causing false alerts.

## Proposed Fix

Route reconciliation queries to the primary database, or add a staleness threshold that waits for replication lag to settle before generating the report.
author: ops-team

comments.author: carol.nguyen
comments.body: We could add a `SELECT pg_last_wal_replay_lsn()` check before running the report. If lag > 5s, wait and retry. This avoids always hitting the primary.

title: Refactor payment module to use dependency injection
body:
## Description

The payment gateway currently hard-codes its dependencies (Redis client, DB repository, state machine). This makes unit testing difficult — every test needs a full integration setup.

## Proposal

Use constructor injection for all dependencies. This also enables the mock source pattern for our CodeSense demo mode.
author: bob.martinez

comments.author: alice.chen
comments.body: Done in PR #89. All payment classes now accept their dependencies via __init__. Test setup is much cleaner — just pass in mocks.
